meta.description: Saturn thesis knowledge graph. Read this first, follow edges to content files.

nodes.desc: Saturn proves zero-config AI provisioning is feasible: five independent mDNS libraries in three languages consume `_saturn._tcp.local.` with no bridging layer, backed by seven total implementations spanning consumer router hardware to npm packages. Literature grounding from Guttman 2001 (zeroconf origins), Siddiqui 2012 (DNS-SD foundations), Siljanovski 2014 (mDNS IoT adaptation), and Kim-Reeves 2020 (DNS deployment footprint).

Saturn reduces total configuration by centralizing complexity in one admin (`saturn run` + TOML) while N consumers call `discover()` with zero keys, URLs, or config files — an asymmetric trade-off where the sysadmin does more so everyone else does nothing. Cognitive walkthrough shows sysadmin 14-26 steps vs traditional 12, but app devs drop from 19 to 4 and end users from 7 payment steps to 0.

Saturn's broadcast trade-offs — passive eavesdropping, plaintext TXT records, no service identity verification — are documented in mDNS literature and mitigated by ephemeral keys (10-min expiry, 5-min rotation) and spending limits. Security coverage ranges from full ephemeral lifecycle (saturn-router Rust `Drop` impl) to zero auth (owui-saturn), deliberately diverging from BeyondCorp zero-trust in favor of zero-config for consumer/campus environments.

Cross-reference of nine Saturn consumer implementations against three thesis claims, showing five independent mDNS libraries and a security-honesty gradient from full ephemeral lifecycle to no auth. Every component achieves zero consumer config through different integration patterns (native SDK, web plugin, MCP tool, subprocess bridge, OpenAI-compatible proxy, CLI env-var wrapper), validating protocol interoperability.

Core Python package: `SaturnAdvertiser` registers `_saturn._tcp.local.` via zeroconf while `discover()` returns priority-sorted `SaturnService` objects with zero client config. Embeds Claim 2 asymmetry — admin complexity in TOML configs, `CredentialManager` (10-min expiry, 5-min rotation), and provider-specific credential handling; consumer path is a single function call.

Rust binary + full OpenWRT integration (procd, UCI, LuCI, shell RPC) turning a GL.iNet GL-MT300N-V2 (~128MB RAM, MIPS32 soft-float) into an mDNS beacon, cross-compiled via Docker to ~2MB binary running from RAM. Strongest evidence for all three claims: infrastructure-layer deployment alongside DHCP/DNS, LuCI form replacing manual key distribution, and full ephemeral lifecycle with Rust `Drop` ensuring key cleanup.

npm package implementing Vercel AI SDK `ProviderV3` that resolves endpoint, auth, and model routing entirely at runtime from mDNS broadcasts — `import { saturn }` starts discovery with zero API keys or env vars. Includes circuit breaker (3 failures → trip → 30s cooldown), auto key refresh on 401, priority-based failover, and self-contained Eliza mock for testing.

Open WebUI 'Pipe' plugin integrating Saturn mDNS/DNS-SD discovery into the ChatGPT-style web interface via Python `zeroconf` ServiceBrowser with TTL-based caching and priority-sorted failover. Fourth independent protocol consumer; admin uploads one file to replace N per-backend configurations, but contains no authentication logic — security boundary is entirely upstream.

Python MCP server (Anthropic's `FastMCP`) exposing Saturn discovery as six tools for AI coding assistants (Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf) — one `mcpServers` JSON entry replaces per-provider API key management. Fifth protocol consumer; `list_available_models` aggregates all discovered services with ephemeral key auth, introducing a trust surface where AI assistants act on users' behalf.

Two-layer integration (Lua VLC extension + Python/FastAPI mDNS bridge packaged as PyInstaller exe) bringing Saturn-discovered AI into VLC — the most consumer-facing component, requiring zero technical knowledge to install and use. Bridge solves VLC's Lua-only limitation via localhost HTTP with URL-encoded JSON; introduces unauthenticated `/shutdown` endpoint and rogue service risk as documented trade-offs.

Fork of OpenCode (Anomaly's AI coding agent) with `ai-sdk-provider-saturn` bundled — 9 commits across 12 files in 4 packages solving dynamic provider registration, fetch timeout/failover, finishReason normalization, and tool-calling flags. Seventh Saturn consumer proving the protocol works in streaming, tool calling, and multi-turn agentic coding while surfacing non-trivial integration challenges.

Jan AI desktop client integrated via local_proxy_client.py — Saturn's OpenAI-compatible proxy makes all discovered services available through Jan's chat interface without any Jan-side modification. Proxy pattern demonstrates Saturn extensibility to unmodified applications.

aider-saturn CLI wrapper discovers Saturn services and launches Aider with OPENAI_BASE_URL/OPENAI_API_KEY pre-configured — zero-config AI pair programming. Supports interactive service/model selection, capability filtering, and ephemeral key pass-through for beacons.

Introduction — equity argument ($20-100/mo paywalls creating access gap), Saturn vision ('AI access like printer access'), thesis statement, and three claims framed tech-first with equity motivating and contextualizing. Opens with the cost problem and positions Saturn as a systems contribution.

Background — mDNS/DNS-SD protocol mechanics (RFCs 6762/6763), rejected alternatives (UPnP, DLNA, WS-Discovery, NetBIOS), AI landscape (AIaaS status quo, OpenAI-compatible API), DHCP analogy, and security context (BeyondCorp, API key leakage, DNS vulnerabilities).

Design — goals (zero-config for consumers, minimal for admins), three target audiences (sysadmin, app dev, end user), beacon architecture (announce don't proxy), TXT record schema, ephemeral key lifecycle, priority routing, and OpenAI-compatible API as endpoint standard.

Implementation — nine components across Python/Rust/TypeScript/Lua: core package, router, AI SDK provider, OWUI plugin, MCP server, VLC extension, OpenCode fork, Jan (via proxy), and Aider (via CLI wrapper), each demonstrating a different integration pattern.
Scenarios — design fiction narrative, VLC extension case study (most consumer-facing component), and 42-target integration analysis showing Saturn's reach across existing AI application ecosystem.

Evaluation — cognitive walkthrough (3 personas, step-count comparison), agent-based discovery trials (C1: 100% success/9.2s), configuration reduction metrics (C2: 65-88% step reduction), and STRIDE threat model + exposure window analysis (C3).

Discussion — 4-scenario threat model (home/campus/coffee shop/compromised), BeyondCorp divergence rationale, API key leakage comparison, AP isolation as hard blocker, and future work (privacy sockets, tiered trust, wide-area discovery).

Conclusion — synthesis of three claims, Saturn as existence proof that network-level AI provisioning works today on commodity hardware, honest limitations (AP isolation, plaintext TXT), and vision for institutional adoption.

Foundational paper on zero-configuration networking — IP connectivity without manual setup, establishing the intellectual roots of mDNS/DNS-SD that Saturn extends to AI service discovery. Demonstrates that network-level auto-configuration is both feasible and widely deployed, directly supporting Claims 1-2.

Survey of zeroconf networking and DNS-based service discovery protocols, establishing mDNS/DNS-SD as mature standardized protocols Saturn builds on. Compares approaches to zero-configuration networking including link-local addressing and multicast name resolution.

Applies mDNS to IoT device discovery, establishing precedent for extending the protocol beyond printers/media to new device domains. Directly supports Claim 1 by demonstrating mDNS is adaptable to new application types, paralleling Saturn's adaptation to AI services.

Comprehensive survey of DNS vulnerabilities and attack vectors (cache poisoning, amplification, tunneling) relevant to Saturn's Claim 3 security analysis. Also documents mDNS's production deployment footprint (printer discovery) as evidence the protocol is mature and production-ready.

Identifies privacy vulnerabilities in mDNS-SD: passive eavesdroppers can enumerate all services and device names without active probing. Directly informs Saturn's Claim 3 threat model — the same exposure applies to `_saturn._tcp.local.` beacons and their TXT metadata.

Proposes efficient privacy-preserving extensions to mDNS-SD using pairwise pre-shared secrets and private service types — a potential future mitigation for Saturn's broadcast exposure. Demonstrates privacy-preserving discovery is possible without abandoning mDNS, supporting the 'addressable' part of Claim 3.

Empirical measurement finding 59% of mDNS device names contain real user names — a concrete privacy leak in production mDNS deployments. Informs Saturn's security discussion: service names and TXT record metadata could similarly leak organizational information.

Introduces Google's BeyondCorp zero-trust architecture — all access requires device verification and per-request authorization regardless of network location. Saturn deliberately diverges: zero-trust overhead is incompatible with zero-config for campus/consumer environments.

BeyondCorp v2: Google's journey from design to deployment of zero-trust, including migration strategies and tiered access control. Suggests a future bridge where Saturn environments could opt into higher trust tiers for sensitive services while keeping zero-config for general use.

Large-scale study of secret leakage in GitHub: 100K+ repos contain valid API keys, 81% of notified developers never remove them, best scanners detect only 25%. Saturn's ephemeral keys directly address this failure class — credentials that auto-expire in 10 minutes cannot be permanently leaked.

Analysis of API security challenges in cloud applications — static key management, insufficient rotation, and organizational inability to track credential inventories. Reinforces Saturn's case for ephemeral credentials: the problem isn't careless developers but a fundamentally broken key management paradigm.

Analysis of AIaaS status quo — subscription models, API key barriers, and per-provider configuration burden creating access fragmentation. Supports Claims 1-2 by documenting the problem Saturn solves: AI is technically available but operationally fragmented across providers, keys, and billing.

Framework for AI democratization across multiple dimensions (access, development, governance) — goes beyond 'more users' to analyze structural barriers. Supports Claim 2's equity framing: reducing configuration burden is one concrete dimension of making AI accessible.

Documents widening socioeconomic effects of AI access inequality — communities without institutional AI infrastructure fall further behind as AI embeds in education and work. Provides empirical backing for Saturn's equity motivation beyond theoretical arguments.

Examines how generative AI adoption in education amplifies existing inequities — students at well-resourced institutions gain advantages while others face subscription paywalls and institutional gaps. Directly motivates Saturn's campus deployment scenario where network-level provisioning equalizes access.

Studies AI adoption at Hispanic-Serving Institutions — budget constraints, infrastructure gaps, and faculty training barriers preventing equitable AI integration. Most specific institutional evidence for Saturn's equity argument, showing real universities face exactly the barriers Saturn targets.

Proposes Chord4S, a decentralized P2P service discovery protocol using DHT-based routing — contrasts with Saturn's mDNS by targeting internet-scale discovery rather than link-local. Demonstrates the design space Saturn intentionally avoids: distributed hash tables solve scale but add complexity incompatible with zero-config.

Vision for distributed, decentralized, and democratized AI via blockchain-based resource sharing and federated learning. Supports Claim 2's democratization framing but contrasts with Saturn's pragmatic approach: Saturn works today on existing networks rather than requiring new infrastructure.

Threat modeling of DNS rebinding attacks in maritime/IoT systems where attackers redirect DNS responses to access internal services. Relevant to Saturn's security model: discovered services could be targeted via rebinding, though ephemeral keys limit the exposure window.

Research on dynamic, context-aware access control that adapts permissions based on environmental factors — theoretical foundation for Saturn's ephemeral key approach as a form of time-limited dynamic authorization. Contrasts with static beacons by showing access control can incorporate context beyond network presence.

Semantic web service discovery in P2P networks using distributed semantic matching — solves broader search problems but requires overlay networks. Contrasts with Saturn: P2P semantic discovery handles unknown service types at scale, while Saturn uses simple mDNS for known `_saturn._tcp.local.` on local networks.

Link-local name resolution (RFC 6762) via multicast to 224.0.0.251:5353 requiring no DNS server — Saturn's protocol foundation, used identically to how Bonjour discovers printers, AirPlay, and Chromecast. Key limitations: AP isolation blocks multicast (confirmed on eduroam/UCSC-guest Jan 2026), passive eavesdropping exposes all announcements, and 59% of device names leak real user names.

DNS-based Service Discovery (RFC 6763) using PTR/SRV/TXT records where Saturn's `_saturn._tcp.local.` TXT schema carries version, api_type, api_base, priority, ephemeral_key, rotation_interval, and features metadata. Runs over mDNS (link-local) or unicast DNS (wide-area); constrained by 255-byte TXT string limit and lacks built-in authentication or encryption.

Time-limited API credentials (10-min lifetime, 5-min rotation with overlap for zero-downtime transitions) distributed via mDNS TXT records and deleted on shutdown, paralleling Kerberos session-layer distribution. Implemented in Python (KeyManager with thread-safe locks) and Rust (full lifecycle with `Drop` impl), making the 'leaked secret never removed' class (Meli 2019: 81%) structurally impossible.

Saturn's announcement architecture: beacons broadcast service metadata and ephemeral credentials via mDNS TXT records without proxying API traffic — clients connect directly to backends, avoiding single-point-of-failure for runtime requests. Three deployment types: cloud (ephemeral keys to cloud APIs), local (local services with health monitoring), and network (shared LAN); proxy clients are architecturally distinct.

Admin-controlled routing preference in mDNS TXT records (lower number = higher priority, mirroring DNS SRV semantics) enabling policy-based model routing — e.g., prefer local Ollama (priority=1) over cloud OpenRouter (priority=10) with automatic failover. Lets institutional IT centrally set routing policy while consumers get the best available service automatically.

DHCP (RFC 2131) is Saturn's closest network-layer analogy — both eliminate manual end-user config via broadcast/multicast and shift complexity to one administrator. Saturn can't use DHCP directly: it provides one-time config at join (Saturn services appear/disappear dynamically), assigns one IP (Saturn discovers N services), requires root (Saturn runs in userspace), and carries minimal metadata.

Google's zero-trust model ('never trust, always verify' with device inventory and per-request access control) that Saturn deliberately diverges from — zero-trust overhead destroys zero-config and is impractical for campus WiFi, coffee shops, and home networks lacking managed devices. Future bridge possible via BeyondCorp v2 and Kaiser-Waldvogel privacy sockets for opt-in tiered trust.

Static API key management is fundamentally broken: 100K+ GitHub repos contain leaked secrets, 81% never removed post-notification, best scanners detect only 25% (Meli 2019, Qazi 2023). Saturn sidesteps this failure class with ephemeral keys that expire in 10 minutes, are never stored in config files/env vars/repos, and exist only transiently via mDNS TXT records.

Universal Plug and Play (SSDP) — rejected for Saturn because it includes device control beyond discovery, has well-documented RCE and port-forwarding vulnerabilities, uses heavyweight SOAP/XML, has declining adoption versus mDNS, and lacks metadata flexibility. Saturn only needs discovery, making UPnP's scope excessive.

Digital Living Network Alliance — media-focused discovery built on UPnP, consortium dissolved 2017. Rejected: media-only (audio/video, not general service discovery), defunct with no active development, inherits UPnP complexity/security issues, provides media transport rather than lightweight metadata exchange.

SOAP-based multicast service discovery used in enterprise environments (Windows network printers) — rejected for Saturn due to verbose SOAP/XML overhead, enterprise orientation for managed networks, limited cross-platform libraries versus mDNS, and no consumer adoption.

Legacy Windows name resolution (NetBIOS over TCP/IP) for SMB file sharing, being phased out by Microsoft in favor of mDNS (Windows 10+ added native mDNS). Rejected: Windows-only, actively deprecated, name resolution only with no service metadata (no TXT equivalent), and known broadcast attack surface.

AI consumption pattern where on-device Whisper transcribes audio and a cloud/local LLM cleans the transcript (SuperWhisper, Wispr Flow, MacWhisper) — illustrates per-user configuration burden multiplying across AI use cases. The DIY path requires configuring two separate AI services manually, which Saturn could provide via single network discovery.

De facto standard REST API (`/v1/chat/completions`, `/v1/models`) implemented by dozens of providers (Ollama, LiteLLM, vLLM, OpenRouter) — Saturn's endpoint compatibility layer. Apps supporting 'custom OpenAI endpoint' work with Saturn immediately; the protocol doesn't prescribe who runs the server, enabling university IT and hobbyists alike.

Anthropic's Model Context Protocol connecting AI assistants to external tools via stdio/HTTP/SSE — the dominant extension protocol for AI coding assistants (Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf). Saturn's `saturn-mcp` is the 5th protocol consumer, exposing discovery as tools where one JSON config entry replaces per-provider key management.

TypeScript framework for AI apps with unified provider-swap API, streaming, tool calling, and structured output — used by Next.js/Vercel AI products. Saturn's `ai-sdk-provider-saturn` is a native `ProviderV3` using `multicast-dns` npm for `_saturn._tcp.local.`, proving cross-language interop (3rd implementation after Python/Rust).
Primary advisor — Computational Media, design automation, HCI; connects to design fiction, democratizing creative tools, friction reduction, and 'the printer test' for Saturn's usability threshold.

Co-advisor — network security, Internet measurement, censorship; connects to mDNS/DNS-SD protocol mechanics, ISP-injected AI threat model, network measurement, and trust/verification in discovered services.

First advisor session (Jan 27, 2026) establishing thesis framing as both networks AND HCI research, with Adam Smith (Computational Media) and Ram Sundara Raman (Internet measurement). Set Winter 2026 UCSC thesis target and LaTeX format.

Second advisor session (Feb 7, 2026) clarifying Saturn is a protocol (not language-specific), beacons announce credentials but don't proxy traffic, and proxy clients are client-side convenience. Details GL.iNet router deployment (MIPS32, Rust, UCI/LuCI), credential rotation (10-min/5-min), and evaluation decision (step-count comparison, no user study).

Core thesis motivation: GenAI paywalls ($20-100/mo) create access inequity — 'AI access ought to be like printer access,' join the network and get capabilities without accounts, credit cards, or API keys. Framed tech-first: Saturn is primarily a systems contribution with equity motivating the Introduction and contextualizing the Discussion.

Development log (Oct 2025 - Jan 2026): protocol definition, naming to Saturn, VLC demo, beacon formalization (ephemeral credentials via mDNS TXT), on-campus mDNS experiment (eduroam/UCSC-guest block multicast, OpenRouter beacon worked on Mango router), soft-float MIPS cross-compile fix, and OpenCode integration bugs.

Three-persona cognitive walkthrough (sysadmin, app developer, end user) evaluated to shared criterion: 'an end user on the network can use an AI-powered feature.' Saturn shifts burden upward (sysadmin 14-26 steps vs 12 traditional) but eliminates it downward (app dev 4 vs 19, end user 0 vs 7 payment steps), reducing total system-wide effort.

Archived automated evaluation (removed Feb 2026) where Claude Code subagents ran blind Docker trials — 5/5 discovery success at ~9.2s, 65% step reduction, 11 metadata fields exposed. Scrapped because Joey delegated too much to the LLM agent and couldn't explain or defend results when Adam asked.

All chapters written (1-8 + appendices A-D + abstract + acknowledgments) with 7 tables and 2 figures in evaluation. Full structure spans equity-motivated introduction through mDNS background, beacon design, 7 client implementations, design fiction/VLC case study/42-target analysis, agent-based evaluation, and 4-scenario threat model discussion.
Winter 2026 target (current quarter) with TODO list for remaining chapters, appendices, and advisor discussions — partially stale since chapter-status now shows all chapters written.

UCSC Graduate Division thesis template: 12pt Times New Roman (mathptmx), double spacing, 1.5'' left margin, Roman numeral prelim pages (title=i hidden, TOC starts iii), Arabic main text from 1, committee signature lines, ProQuest PDF submission.

Saturn's external engagement: Reddit r/RASPBERRY_PI_PROJECTS and r/OpenWebUI (feedback pointed to LiteLLM/Requesty as alternatives), Ollama GitHub #10283, Clad Labs/Mozilla contacts (no response), MiniStS class integration, and competitor landscape (LiteLLM, Requesty, RouteLLM, ANS, A2A).

Fall 2025 quarterly report: 'too cheap to meter' framing, cost problem ($20/mo subscriptions, per-app API keys, open-source devs can't force cost-bearing), Saturn as mDNS+DNS-SD discovery with Bonjour printer analogy. Notes 'I struggled to find any implementation as extensive as Saturn' against LiteLLM, Requesty, Ollama PR #751.

Pedagogical 10-rung concept ladder from the access problem through mDNS/DNS-SD foundations, beacon architecture, ephemeral keys, and implementation evidence up to the three thesis claims. Designed as an onboarding tool for readers unfamiliar with Saturn's design space.

Seven implementations across Python, Rust, TypeScript, OWUI, MCP, VLC Lua/bridge, and OpenCode consume `_saturn._tcp.local.` using five different mDNS libraries, with API-agnostic discovery supporting Ollama, DeepInfra JWTs, and OpenRouter ephemeral keys via `api_type` TXT. Hardware deployment proven on GL.iNet GL-MT300N-V2 (MIPS32, 128MB RAM, ~2MB Rust binary).

Hard blockers: AP isolation (confirmed — eduroam/UCSC-guest block multicast), VLANs without multicast bridging, firewalls blocking UDP 5353. Soft blockers: no service identity verification (rogue services appear legitimate), plaintext TXT key exposure to all LAN devices, RAM-only router deployment lost on reboot, and 1-5 second discovery latency.

Enterprise/institutional WiFi blocks multicast between clients (AP isolation). Confirmed Jan 2026: eduroam and UCSC-guest both block mDNS. Saturn's hardest deployment blocker — directly undermines campus scenario. Not solvable within protocol; requires network administrator action.

Saturn fully supports text chat completions and agentic coding across all backends/clients; vision/embeddings/audio work as passthrough but aren't advertised in TXT records. Image generation, WebSocket APIs, and TTS don't work today — the limitation is practical (implementations target chat) not architectural (discovery is modality-agnostic).

Claude (Anthropic) serving as iterative thesis advisor — spotting inconsistencies, articulating ideas, challenging assumptions, with explicit boundaries where human judgment overrides AI. Three documented rejected suggestions: fourth cost claim, Monte Carlo simulation, quantum threats.

Moons knowledge graph authored iteratively with Claude across four phases: scaffold definition (Oct 2025), bulk edge generation (Nov-Dec, ~100 candidates per session), refinement passes (Jan-Feb 2026), and live updates after meetings. Claude excelled at cross-domain connections but couldn't verify accuracy or resolve contradictions — human reviewed every edge.

AI's role in evaluation design — Claude assisted with cognitive walkthrough validation, STRIDE threat model generation, grader prompt refinement, and result interpretation. Documents the boundary between AI as design partner (proposing evaluation structure) versus AI as evaluator (explicitly avoided).

Three roles AI played in thesis development: authoring moons knowledge graph via iterative prompting, serving as thesis advisor (conceptual feedback, argument coherence), and supporting evaluation design (STRIDE validation, grader prompts). Documents the distinction between AI as amplifier versus AI as author.

Extended appendix on AI's role across four parts: moons authorship process, Claude as advisor (with three rejected suggestion examples), AI in evaluation design, and explicit decision boundaries where human judgment overrode AI. Transparent methodology disclosure for reproducibility and academic integrity.

Nielsen's Ten Usability Heuristics applied to Saturn. Strengths: visibility (H1), error prevention via failover (H5), recognition over recall via discover() (H6), flexibility (H7). Trade-offs: sysadmin terminology confusion (H2), role-dependent control (H3), TXT schema drift across implementations (H4). Limitations: weak error recovery (H9), no debugging guidance.

desc: Reusable prompt for subagents to perform advisor-level structural review of thesis sections. Distilled from Adam's live feedback on the Introduction: missing contributions list, artifact vs evaluative claim distinction, ordering by pedagogical value, design-intent framing, honest trade-off statement, value density. Six checks, no prose polish.
